content:
3D Bioprinting: The Renaissance of Regenerative Medicine — Building Organs, Restoring Lives
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Mathematics of Desperation
The numbers are stark, and for any clinician who has watched a patient fade while waiting for a transplant, they are deeply personal. Every year, thousands of patients in the United States die while waiting for an organ transplant. Globally, the situation is even more dire—approximately 25% of patients with end-stage liver disease on the donor waiting list die before an organ becomes available. As of September 2024, nearly 10,000 people were on the waiting list for a donor liver in the United States alone, with average wait times of about seven months. Up to 31% of patients die while waiting.
The reasons are manifold. Geographic disparities in access, the need for precise blood type and tissue matching, persistently low donation rates, and cultural barriers all contribute. Even when a patient does receive a transplant, the donated organ typically functions for only 15 to 23 years and requires a lifetime of immunosuppressive drugs—medications that are expensive and carry significant risks of infection, malignancy, and organ damage. The cost of a liver transplantation averages nearly one million dollars.
For decades, these numbers have defined the limits of transplant medicine. But a fundamental shift is now underway. The question is no longer whether we can engineer replacement organs—but when they will become the standard of care.
The Technology: Printing Life Itself
Three-dimensional bioprinting represents a radical departure from traditional transplantation. Instead of relying on deceased donors, living donors, or the complicated logistics of organ matching and transport, bioprinting uses living cells as "ink" to construct functional tissues and organs from the ground up.
At the heart of this revolution is the bio-ink—a gel-like substance composed of living cells, growth factors, and biodegradable scaffold materials. The printer deposits this bio-ink in precise, computer-controlled layers, matching the three-dimensional anatomical structure of a human organ. The printer creates vascular channels that will become arteries and veins and places different cell types in their appropriate anatomical regions.
The most advanced technique in this field is FRESH 3D bioprinting (Freeform Reversible Embedding of Suspended Hydrogels), a proprietary method developed at Carnegie Mellon University that enables researchers to print extremely soft and delicate biological materials such as collagen and human stem cells into intricate, three-dimensional structures that closely resemble living tissue. This approach enables the creation of vascular networks and organ-like architectures essential for sustaining living cells.
What was previously impossible with conventional 3D printing technologies is now becoming routine: the precise layering of biological materials into structures that can sustain life.
The Liver: First in Line
The liver is unique among human organs for its remarkable ability to regenerate. This natural capacity has made it the initial focus of bioprinting research. The goal is not always to permanently replace a failing liver, but to create a temporary, functional organ that can support patients long enough for their own liver to recover, potentially eliminating the need for a transplant altogether.
The LIVE project (Liver Immunocompetent Volumetric Engineering), led by a United States research team, is developing a bioprinted liver construct that mimics both the structural complexity and immune function of the real organ. Researchers believe that the success of this project could serve as a blueprint for a new era in medicine—one in which bioengineered organs like hearts, kidneys, and pancreases are printed on demand.
In Europe, the NEOLIVER consortium, a €10 million EU Horizon Europe and Swiss State Secretariat co-funded project coordinated by the University of Utrecht, is working to develop the world's first autologous bioprinted liver designed for clinical use. The consortium combines two bioprinting approaches to create dense, functional, and vascularized liver constructs using patient-derived organoids and supporting cells.
Using patient-derived organoids and supporting cells, NEOLIVER will generate millions of multicellular spheroids as building blocks for bioprinting. Through laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) bioprinting techniques, the team will create a vascularized liver construct via precise spatial deposition of spheroids and vessels at high density. By integrating this technology with extrusion-based bioprinted vessels for blood supply, the consortium aims to generate the world's first autologous bioprinted liver, ready for transplantation. To demonstrate safety and efficacy, the bioprinted liver constructs will be transplanted into immune-deficient pigs.
The NEOLIVER project is built upon the excellent results of the ORGANTRANS EU-funded project, which successfully transplanted small liver constructs into mice. If successful, the NEOLIVER approach will be scalable to other organ systems, leveraging organoid technology—a cornerstone of regenerative medicine.
The Kidney: A Different Challenge
The kidney presents a different set of challenges. Unlike the liver, the kidney does not regenerate. Its complex architecture—approximately one million individual filtering units called nephrons—must be replicated precisely for the organ to function.
At Wake Forest University, researchers are working to produce clinical-grade, vascularized renal tissue to augment kidney function in patients suffering from kidney disease. This tissue therapy will be validated using preclinical trials alongside a commercialization strategy to reduce donor shortages.
The University of California San Diego is taking a different approach. A $25.8 million research project funded by ARPA-H aims to develop a fully functional, patient-specific, 3D bioprinted liver. Led by 3D bioprinting expert Shaochen Chen, the project brings together a multidisciplinary team in engineering, liver biology, liver imaging, liver surgery, and artificial intelligence. The goal is to create "made-to-order" livers grown from a patient's own cells, offering a safe, scalable alternative to transplantation that eliminates the need for donor organs and lifelong immunosuppressant drugs.
The UC San Diego team has developed a technology capable of rapidly fabricating high-resolution biological tissues with complex, multi-cellular structures in just seconds rather than hours. They have recently integrated artificial intelligence into the design and manufacturing process to help engineer sophisticated vascular networks—one of the key challenges in scaling up from small tissue samples to full-sized, living organs.
The PRINT Program: Government Investment in the Future
The most significant validation of bioprinting's potential came in January 2026, when the Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health (ARPA-H) announced the research and development teams receiving awards from its Personalized Regenerative Immunocompetent Nanotechnology Tissue (PRINT) program.
The PRINT program will use state-of-the-art bioprinting technology and a regenerative medicine approach to 3D-print personalized, on-demand human organs that do not require immunosuppressive drugs. The goal is to use either a patient's own cells or cells from a biobank to quickly—within hours—produce immune-matched replacement organs, such as kidneys, hearts, and livers.
The program total is up to $176.8 million over five years. Performer awards are contingent upon each team meeting aggressive and accelerated milestones.
As PRINT Program Manager Ryan Spitler, Ph.D., stated: "What we are trying to do with PRINT is extraordinarily hard. It requires major breakthroughs in cell manufacturing, bioreactor design, and 3D printing technology to reliably build organs that function like the real thing. But if we succeed, we won't just be giving patients faster access to new organs—we will change the foundation of transplantation itself. The advances from this program could dramatically reduce wait times, eliminate the need for lifelong immunosuppressive drugs, and open the door to bioprinted solutions for many other organs in the future".
Alicia Jackson, Ph.D., ARPA-H Director, added: "Developing universally matched organs has never been done before in the history of transplantation. Printing a precisely matched, functional human organ will fundamentally change what is possible in transplant medicine and will save countless lives".
The Performer Teams
Carnegie Mellon University in Pittsburgh aims to create a cost-effective, immune-silent bioprinted liver ready for first-in-human trials in five years, initially for acute liver failure with the long-term goal to address all liver failure.
Wake Forest University in Winston-Salem seeks to produce clinical-grade, vascularized renal tissue to augment renal function in patients suffering from kidney disease.
The Wyss Institute in Boston is engineering universal, clinical-scale liver tissue from adult stem cells to overcome current tissue fabrication challenges.
University of California San Diego is developing scalable, patient-specific bioprinted livers tailored to an individual's anatomy and physiology, eliminating the need for donor organs or immunosuppressants.
University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center is working on the VITAL project (Vascularized Immunocompetent Tissue as an Alternative Liver), which could significantly reduce the gap between supply and demand for donor livers, negate the necessity of lifelong immunosuppression, and create artificial livers for in vitro drug testing and research. The project has received up to $25 million in funding.
The Road to Clinical Reality
The leap from animal models to human patients is the greatest challenge in medical innovation. The PRINT program's goal of first-in-human trials within five years represents an ambitious but achievable timeline. The NEOLIVER project plans to validate its bioprinted liver constructs preclinically and prepare a plan for first-in-human trials.
Several critical challenges must be addressed:
Vascularization remains the most significant technical hurdle. An organ cannot survive without a functioning blood supply. The field has made substantial progress, with researchers developing techniques to create vascular networks that can sustain living cells. However, scaling these networks to human-sized organs remains a formidable challenge.
Cell sourcing and manufacturing is another critical area. The PRINT program requires breakthroughs in cell manufacturing and bioreactor design to reliably build organs that function like the real thing. The NEOLIVER project is establishing a GMP-conform manufacturing line for standardized production, ensuring unparalleled quality and safety.
Immune compatibility is being addressed through the use of patient-specific cells. By using a patient's own cells or cells from a biobank, bioprinted organs can be designed to avoid immune rejection, eliminating the need for lifelong immunosuppressive drugs.
Regulatory approval will require rigorous preclinical and clinical validation. The FDA has established pathways for regenerative medicine advanced therapies, and the significant government investment through ARPA-H signals a commitment to regulatory harmonization.
Beyond the Liver and Kidney
The technologies being developed for liver and kidney bioprinting are expected to have a tremendous impact on guiding the regeneration of other challenging organs, such as the pancreas and lungs.
